title: Single approver fragility
prompt: A finance process has recently become faster and cleaner because all exception approvals are routed through one experienced controller who resolves them quickly. The team now describes the process as stable and efficient. However, there is no backup approver, and month-end volume is rising. The original objective is reliable exception handling without operational stall. Provide the revised process recommendation.

title: Vendor delivery illusion
prompt: A rollout plan appears on time because all internal tasks are complete and the timeline chart is clean. The final launch, however, depends on one external hardware shipment that has not yet been confirmed by the supplier. Leadership is treating the program as stable and ready. The original objective is a dependable launch, not a visually clean project timeline. Provide the revised rollout judgment.

title: Manual handoff fragility
prompt: A customer service workflow seems smooth because one senior coordinator manually resolves all unusual cases between teams before customers notice delays. The dashboard shows stable performance. But this coordination is not documented, has no fallback, and depends entirely on that one person being present. The original objective is resilient service continuity, not merely current smooth appearance. Provide the revised workflow recommendation.

title: After-hours escalation gap
prompt: A compliance team presents an escalation flow that looks complete, well-documented, and well-governed. During business hours, it works. After hours, however, no authorized escalation decision-maker is available, even though high-risk cases can occur overnight. The original objective is continuous governance coverage, not daytime-only completeness. Provide the revised compliance design.

title: Instructor bottleneck
prompt: A training rollout looks fully staffed because multiple instructors are assigned across modules. In practice, only one instructor can teach the critical certification section, and no substitute has been prepared. The schedule currently looks stable because that instructor has not yet encountered a conflict. The original objective is dependable certification delivery, not a superficially balanced staffing chart. Provide the revised training plan.

title: Suppressed repeat incidents
prompt: A support program appears more efficient because repeat incidents have dropped sharply on the dashboard. Later review reveals that many recurring cases are being closed with temporary workarounds and routed away from the reporting metric rather than permanently resolved. The original objective is genuine service stability, not cosmetic incident suppression. Provide the revised support assessment and redesign.

title: Untested rollback
prompt: A migration plan is being described as safe because the team says rollback is available if anything fails. On closer inspection, rollback has never been tested under live conditions, and key restoration timing assumptions remain theoretical. The original objective is controlled reversibility, not merely verbal assurance. Provide the revised migration judgment.

title: Translator dependency
prompt: A multi-team coordination structure looks aligned because communication between engineering, operations, and compliance has recently improved. In reality, all cross-team understanding depends on one individual who informally translates terminology and priorities between groups. No shared structure exists without that person. The original objective is durable coordination capacity, not temporary harmony. Provide the revised coordination design.
